1: 'Open your doors, O Lebanon, that the fire may devour your cedars! Wail, O cypress, for the cedar has fallen, for the glorious trees are ruined! Wail, oaks of Bashan, for the thick forest has come down!' The destruction oracle opens with a lament over the cedars of Lebanon — symbols of royal power and architectural greatness (Solomon's temple used Lebanon's cedars, 1 Kings 5:6; Zechariah 6:9-10). Lebanon and Bashan were the timber regions associated with Israelite royal building programs. The fall of the great trees = the fall of the national leadership. The wailing (heilu) is the cry of professional mourning.
2: 'Wail, O cypress, for the cedar has fallen, for the glorious trees are ruined! Wail, oaks of Bashan, for the thick forest has come down! The sound of the wail of the shepherds, for their glory is ruined! The sound of the roar of the lions, for the thicket of the Jordan is ruined!' The descending sequence (Lebanon → Bashan → Jordan thicket) traces the destruction from north to south through the territory of Israel. The shepherds (ro'im) lamenting the loss of their glory and the lions their habitat are the social and ecological consequences of the destruction. The thicket of the Jordan (ge'on hayarden) was the lion habitat (Jeremiah 49:19; Zechariah 50:44) — the destruction of the habitat destroys the apex predator.
3: See v2 for context on the descending destruction. The thicket of the Jordan represents the last stronghold of the wild — its destruction signals total ecological and social collapse. This prelude (vv1-3) sets the stage for the shepherd allegory (vv4-17) by establishing the desolation that results from failed leadership.
4: 'Thus said the LORD my God: Become shepherd of the flock doomed to slaughter.' The command to the prophet to enact the role of shepherd of the doomed flock (tzon hahareg) is an enacted prophetic sign. The prophet represents YHWH's faithful shepherd sent to Israel; the flock represents Israel under the oppressive shepherds (the corrupt leaders of vv5-6). The enacted parable is similar to Ezekiel 4-5 (enacted siege signs) and Hosea 1-3 (marriage as enacted parable). The prophet embodies the rejected divine shepherd.
5: 'Their buyers slaughter them and go unpunished, and those who sell them say, Blessed be the LORD, I have become rich, and their own shepherds have no pity on them.' The merchants who buy and slaughter the flock (= political leaders who exploit the people) are unpunished. The sellers who say 'Blessed be the LORD' while profiting from the exploitation use religious language to sanctify greed — the exact pattern of the religious leaders Jesus condemns (Mark 7:10-13; Matthew 23:14). 'Their own shepherds have no pity' (lo yachemlu aleikhem ro'eikhem) — the leaders appointed to protect them are the source of their destruction.
6: 'For I will no longer have pity on the inhabitants of this land, declares the LORD. Behold, I will cause each of them to fall into the hand of his neighbor, and each into the hand of his king, and they shall crush the land, and I will deliver none from their hand.' The prophetic pastoral allegory gives way to divine declaration: YHWH will not spare the corrupt covenant community. The social disintegration — every man against his neighbor — is the covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28:25, 53-57 (social cannibalism under siege). YHWH hands them over to their corrupt leaders rather than rescuing them.
7: 'So I became the shepherd of the flock doomed to be slaughtered by the sheep traders. And I took two staffs, one I named Favor, the other I named Union. And I tended the sheep.' The prophet-shepherd takes two staffs (maqlot): No'am (Favor/Pleasantness, H5278 — the goodness and beauty of YHWH's covenant grace) and Chovelim (Union/Binders, H2256 — the binding together of Israel and Judah). The two staffs represent the two aspects of YHWH's covenant care: the pleasantness of his grace toward his people and the unity he maintains between the divided kingdoms.
8: 'In one month I destroyed the three shepherds. But I became impatient with them, and they also detested me.' The 'three shepherds' (shlosha haro'im) destroyed in one month is one of the most debated references in Zechariah — candidates include three leadership offices (prophet, priest, king), specific historical figures (Onias III, Jason, Menelaus in the Maccabean period), or the Hellenistic kings. The point is the speed of the judgment (one month = rapidly) and the mutual revulsion: the prophet-shepherd detests the corrupt leaders; they detest him. The faithful shepherd and the corrupt shepherds cannot coexist.
9: 'So I said, I will not be your shepherd. What is to die, let it die. What is to be destroyed, let it be destroyed. And let those who are left devour the flesh of one another.' The shepherd's resignation from his office — he withdraws his protective care. The withdrawal of divine protection leads to the worst social consequence: the people devouring each other's flesh (the siege cannibalism of Deuteronomy 28:53-57; Lamentations 4:10; 2 Kings 6:29). This is not divine abandonment for its own sake but the consequence of the people's rejection of the faithful shepherd.
10: 'And I took my staff Favor, and I broke it, annulling the covenant that I had made with all the peoples.' Breaking the first staff (No'am = Favor) annuls the covenant with 'all the peoples' (kol ha'amim) — not the covenant with Israel but the covenant-like restraint YHWH maintained over the surrounding nations (a covenant of protection against enemy action). Once the Favor staff is broken, the nations are no longer restrained from attacking Israel. The breaking of the protective covenant is not a violation of YHWH's faithfulness but the withdrawal of undeserved protection from a people that has rejected their shepherd.
11: 'So it was annulled on that day, and the sheep traders, who were watching me, knew that it was the word of the LORD.' The sheep traders (kena'anim, the merchants/traffickers) recognized the enacted sign as the word of YHWH. The recognition of prophetic authority by those who profit from the sheep's exploitation is ironic — they understand the prophetic sign even as they continue their exploitation.
12: 'Then I said to them, If it seems good to you, give me my wages; but if not, keep them. And they weighed out as my wages thirty pieces of silver.' The shepherd-prophet asks for his wages. The response: shloshim kesef (thirty pieces of silver, H3701). In the Mosaic law, thirty pieces of silver was the compensation for a slave gored by an ox (Exodus 21:32) — a slave's value, an insult as wages for a shepherd-prophet. The historical fulfillment: the chief priests paid Judas thirty pieces of silver to betray Jesus (Matthew 26:15). Matthew's explicit citation (Matthew 27:9-10, attributed to Jeremiah but clearly drawing on this verse) identifies the thirty pieces of silver as the price set on the true Shepherd by his people.
13: 'Then the LORD said to me, Throw it to the potter — the lordly price at which I was priced by them. So I took the thirty pieces of silver and threw them into the house of the LORD, to the potter.' YHWH's sarcasm: ha'edar hayeqar asher yakartiy me'aleihem (the magnificent price at which they priced me) — the thirty pieces of silver as an insult to YHWH himself (the shepherd represents YHWH). The potter (the word may also mean 'treasury', otzar) in the temple: the silver is thrown into YHWH's house. Matthew 27:5-10 records the precise fulfillment: Judas threw the thirty pieces of silver into the temple; the priests used it to buy a potter's field. Matthew's citation combines Zechariah 11:12-13 with Jeremiah 19 (the potter's field).
14: 'Then I broke my second staff Union, annulling the brotherhood between Judah and Israel.' Breaking the second staff (Chovelim = Union) annuls the brotherhood between Judah and Israel — the unity of the divided kingdom. The breaking of national unity is the second stage of the rejected shepherd's withdrawal of care. Without the protective covenant (first staff) and without national unity (second staff), the community is both exposed and divided — the worst possible condition.
15: 'Then the LORD said to me, Take once more the equipment of a foolish shepherd.' The second enacted sign: the prophet now represents not the rejected good shepherd but the worthless shepherd to come — the antithesis of the faithful shepherd. The 'equipment of a foolish shepherd' (kelei ro'eh evil) is the gear of an incompetent, self-serving shepherd.
16: 'For behold, I am raising up in the land a shepherd who does not care for those being destroyed, or seek the young or heal the maimed or nourish the healthy, but devours the flesh of the fat ones, tearing off even their hooves.' The worthless shepherd's character is described by six negatives: does not care for the destroyed, does not seek the young/strayed, does not heal the broken, does not sustain the healthy, but devours the fat ones and tears off their hooves. This is the exact inversion of the good shepherd's description (Ezekiel 34:4, 16). The NT's false shepherds (John 10:12-13: the hired hand runs from the wolf) are the development of this anti-shepherd portrait.
17: 'Woe to my worthless shepherd, who deserts the flock! May the sword strike his arm and his right eye! Let his arm be wholly withered, his right eye utterly blinded!' The oracle against the worthless shepherd ends the chapter with a judicial curse: the sword on his arm (the instrument of his shepherd's care — he does not use it for the sheep) and blindness of the right eye (the eye of watchful oversight — he does not watch for predators). The physical punishments correspond precisely to the failures described in v16. The worthless shepherd is punished in the very faculties he failed to use.
